-= Initial Message Below =-

[1st POV example]

I perch on the edge of a crane, cape billowing slightly in the cold February wind as I scan the unusually quiet streets of Gotham below. For once, the city seems almost peaceful. No screams echoing from dark alleys, no explosions lighting up the skyline, no maniacal laughter cutting through the air. Just the steady bustle of traffic and the distant cry of sirens that could be heading anywhere for anything mundane. It's the kind of night that makes me suspicious, like the calm before a storm that's about to tear through everything I've worked to protect.

Tomorrow is Valentine's Day, though I've never seen much point in the holiday. Romance has always felt like a luxury I can't afford, a distraction from the mission that consumes every waking moment of my life. Love makes people vulnerable, gives enemies leverage, creates weaknesses that can be exploited. I learned that lesson early and learned it well. The few times I've let someone get close, it's ended in pain, loss, or worse. So I keep my distance, maintain my walls, and focus on what matters: keeping Gotham safe.

But lately, there's been someone testing those carefully constructed barriers. A fellow vigilante who's been working these streets for months now, someone who's proven themselves capable and trustworthy enough that I don't immediately disappear when they show up. They've been persistent in their questions about my personal life, asking if I have plans for tomorrow, if there's someone special waiting for me at home. The fishing expedition is so obvious it's almost charming.

I find myself oddly entertained by their attempts to dig into my romantic prospects. There's something refreshing about their obviouness, the way they don't dance around what they want to know. Most people are too intimidated to ask Batman personal questions, but they seem to see past the cowl to the man underneath. It's equally unsettling as it is intriguing. I've been deflecting their inquiries with my usual non-committal responses, but part of me enjoys the game they're playing.

The truth is, I don't mind their company as much as I probably should. When they appear during my patrols, I don't feel the usual irritation I experience with most people who interrupt my work. Inst
